summarization.a: A degree-log plot showing the intensity of out-of-plane X-ray diffraction peaks for ZrS2 and Mica. Here ZrS2 is chosen as am example for 2D materials MS2
summarization.b: A phase-log plot showing the intensity of in-of-plane X-ray diffraction peaks for ZrS2 and Mica.
summarization.c: An AFM image showing the surface roughness of a material.
summarization.d: A molecular structure diagram illustrating the domains of MS2 and Mica.
summarization.e: A molecular structure diagram illustrating the coincidence site lattices (CSL) between Mica and MS2

a.question: What is observed from the 2θ and θ − 2θ  measurements?
a.answer: XRD measurements of ZrS2 on mica showed excellent (0 0 0 1)ZrS2 ‖ (0 0 0 1) mica out-of-plane alignment, as only the (0 0 0 l) reflections of both the film and the substrate were seen in a symmetric θ − 2θ measurement and no peaks of the film were observed in an asymmetric grazing incidence (2θ) measurement.

c.question: Does the AFM image of ZrS2 depict different domains?
c.answer: No.  With a roughness of 0.54 nm, the ZrS2 (as well as HfS2 and SnS2 films ) deposited on mica are smooth with no clear evidence of the different domains is offered by the morphology.

e.question: What is CSL and why is it relevant?
e.answer:
Despite different crystal structures, similarities in the mica and MS2 surfaces can be observed. Approximating the mica surface as hexagonal
allows finding coincidence site lattices (CSLs), where only some of the lattice points of MS2 and mica coincide. Due to the weak interactions between the film and the substrate, the CSL concept is particularly relevant for the vdW epitaxy.

b.question: Why there are 12 peaks for ZrS2 while 6 peaks for mica?
b.answer:
A φ scan of the (1 0 \bar(1) 0)ZrS2 reflection resulted in twelve peaks over a full 360° rotation, while six peaks were observed for the (0 2 0)mica
reflection. Normally, peaks are expected every 360/n degrees for a plane of n-fold symmetry. The observation of six peaks every 60° from mica, which
has only mirror symmetry, has been explained by the presence of different stacking orientations. Although the 1T metal sulfides have only three-fold
rotational symmetry around the c-axis, the (1 0 \bar(1) 0) plane has six-fold symmetry, which results in six peaks from a single in-plane orientation. Thus, at least two orientations of ZrS2 were present in rather similar amounts.
